concerns.text: Acceptance Scenario 1 requires 'at least 95% of the original records' to be matched. This is a specific empirical threshold (95%) that cannot be verified in the planning stage without running the ingestion. Per the [deferred] convention and SC-001, this should be a measurement definition (e.g., 'percentage is measured against total valid records'), not a fixed pass/fail number.

The phrase 'reasonable geographic range (e.g., -50°C to 60°C)' in the Independent Test mixes a test procedure with a specific value. While the range is plausible, defining 'reasonable' as a fixed numeric bound in a spec without a cited source (e.g., NOAA valid range documentation) makes it an arbitrary implementation constraint rather than a verifiable requirement derived from data properties.

SC-005 states: 'The proportion of model residuals falling within the 95% confidence interval... is measured against the theoretical expectation'. This is unmeasurable as written because 'theoretical expectation' is not defined (e.g., is it 95%? 90%? 99%?). A success criterion must state the specific threshold (e.g., 'measured against 95%') or the statistical test used (e.g., 'measured against the Kolmogorov-Smirnov p-value > 0.05').

The Edge Case 'What happens when the NOAA station nearest... is >100km away?' proposes a specific distance threshold (100km) as a system behavior. This is an unverified empirical assumption. The spec should define the *metric* to be measured (e.g., 'distance to nearest station is measured against a configurable threshold') rather than hard-coding the threshold value in the requirement text, as the optimal threshold depends on data density.
